title: Minimalist Modern Dresser
summary: A six-drawer modern dresser with beveled plank drawer faces — a 1x3 face frame carrying corner-mount slides, plywood sides, and drawer boxes cut to fit their real openings.
description:
This is a knock-off of a catalog dresser at a fraction of the price, and its structure is unusual in a way worth understanding: the FRONT FACE FRAME is the drawer-slide foundation. Six bottom-corner-mount slides (the cheap white ones, chosen on purpose — they're forgiving and invisible here) register off the 1x3 frame, which is why step 1 hammers the squaring lesson so hard: measure both diagonals, and if they disagree, push the long corners together until they match. A parallelogram face frame means six drawers that all bind the same direction.

The drawer math in step 8 is the most transferable skill in the plan. Sides are cut to match the slide length (15-3/4" for 16" slides). Fronts and backs are the measured opening minus twice the slide clearance (23" opening, 1/2" per side → 20-1/2"). And the source's build-ONE-first rule is the difference between an evening of fitting and a weekend of re-cutting: slightly small adjusts out via the slides' slotted screw holes; slightly big meets the saw again.

The faces are the design statement: 1x10s ripped to 8-1/2" with a 45° bevel on the top edge of each. The bevels create shadow lines that read as drawer pulls — there is no hardware on this dresser, you grip the bevel. Install the bottom faces first, then use a ripped 1-1/8" spacer strip to set each next face's gap; a consistent reveal is the whole minimalist look.

One safety item the plan can't skip: a 50"-wide six-drawer dresser is a tip hazard with all drawers open. Add an anti-tip strap to the wall stud — furniture this size gets climbed by someone eventually.

tools.note: Ripping the 1x10 faces to 8-1/2" with the 45° bevel — the design detail of the whole piece.
1-1/4" and 5/8" brads — the whole carcass is brads and glue.
Slide installation and securing faces from behind.

materials.name: 1x10, 8 ft
materials.note: Drawer faces — ripped to 8-1/2" with the beveled top edge.

materials.name: Plywood, 3/4", cut into two 16" x 8 ft strips
materials.note: Sides and top panel. Have the store rip the sheet.

materials.name: Drawer slides, 16", bottom corner mount
materials.note: The usually-white ones. Cheap, adjustable, hidden — correct for this design.

materials.name: Anti-tip strap
materials.note: Into a wall stud. Six open drawers is a ladder to a toddler.

cutList.note: Matched to the 16" slides. Fronts/backs and bottoms are CUT TO FIT per step 5's math.
Ripped to 8-1/2", top edge at 45° bevel. Final length cut to your openings with 1/8" reveals.

steps.title: Build the face frame square — actually square
steps.body:
Assemble the 1x3 face frame with glue and 1-1/4" brads, three per joint. Then measure BOTH diagonals. If they differ, push the two long-diagonal corners toward each other and re-measure until they match.

The drawer slides register off this frame. A frame that's 1/4" out of square isn't 1/4" of charm — it's six drawers that all rub. Add the 1x2 center divider dead-centered with its blocking pieces.

steps.title: Sides onto the frame
steps.body:
On each 16" plywood side, attach a 1x3 flush to the bottom and back edges (it will stand 3/4" proud at the top — that's planned). Then nail the sides to the face frame, front and bottom edges flush.

Set the dresser on a level surface and add the bottom trim across the front and back.
materials: Plywood, 3/4", cut into two 16" x 8 ft strips

steps.title: Back and front rails
steps.body: Add the 29" verticals: 1x3s on the BACK edges of the plywood, 1x2s on the FRONT. Glue and brads. These close the side silhouettes and give the top frame its landing.

steps.title: Build and land the top
steps.body:
Frame the top panel — 16" 1x2s on the ends first, then 52" 1x2s front and back — with glue and 1-1/4" brads. Set the completed top onto the dresser and nail in every direction you can reach.

The source is right to make this sound aggressive: the top is the squaring diaphragm for the whole carcass. Then center the 2x2 support vertically on the back and glue-and-nail it — it stops the long top from ever sagging.
materials: Plywood, 3/4", cut into two 16" x 8 ft strips

steps.title: Drawer math, then ONE test drawer
steps.body:
Sides: 15-3/4", matching the slides. Fronts/backs: measure your actual opening (should be ~23"), confirm your slides want 1/2" per side, cut to opening minus 2-1/2" of total clearance and material (20-1/2" here). Bottoms: cut 1/4" ply to the assembled box.

Build ONE drawer — glue and 1-1/4" brads, bottom on with 5/8" brads — mount its hardware, and test it in the cabinet before building five more. Err small: the slides' oval adjustment holes forgive small, not large.

steps.title: Install slides and remaining drawers
steps.body:
Slide members mount to the drawer bottoms — wheels to the back, flush to the front — screwed through the widthwise oval holes so side-to-side adjustment stays live. Cabinet members mount with their front edges matched to the face frame's 1x3 side returns and the 1x2 center blocking.

Test, adjust, then production-line the remaining five drawers.

steps.title: Bevel-ripped faces with a spacer reveal
steps.body:
Rip the 1x10s to 8-1/2" with the top edge beveled at 45° — the bevel IS the drawer pull; there's no hardware to buy or align. Cut faces to your openings allowing ~1/8" between.

Install the bottom row first: brads from the front to position, screws from inside the drawer to hold. Then rip a 1-1/8" spacer strip, rest each next face on it, and attach — the spacer makes every reveal identical, and identical reveals are the entire minimalist effect.

Fill, sand, stain, finish — and strap the dresser to a wall stud. Six drawers open is a staircase to anyone under four.

images.alt: Minimalist six-drawer modern dresser with beveled plank drawer faces
